segments.s500-g217: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. The phrase has subject + temporal marker + object + verb order typical of Persian poetry: 'سکندر' (subject) + 'چو آن پاسخ‌نامه' (when that response-letter) + 'دید' (past verb 'saw'). 'آن' is the demonstrative modifying 'پاسخ‌نامه'.
segments.s500-g218: This is a short past clause whose subject (سکندر) is carried over from the previous segment; 'خردمند و بینادلی' are adjectives used substantively ('the wise and the sincere') and coordinated by و, serving as the direct object of the past verb 'برگزید' ('chose').
segments.s500-g219: بدیشان is a dative prepositional phrase 'to them'; 'پیامی' is the indefinite object 'a message'; 'فرستاد' is the simple past 'sent'; 'و گفت' ('and said') links to the reported content introduced by the following 'که'.
segments.s500-g220: که introduces the content clause 'that...'; 'با مغز مردم' is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with the minds of people'; 'خرد' is the theme 'wisdom'; 'باد' is the optative particle ('may it be') used in classical Persian to express a wish; 'جفت' (literally 'pair') acts as predicate meaning 'be paired/put in partnership'. Word order places the verb-like optative particle after the subject-predicate elements.
segments.s500-g221: به گرد جهان is an adverbial phrase 'throughout/about the world' (به + گرد + جهان); 'شهریاری' is the abstract noun 'sovereignty/kingship'; 'نماند' is the past negative 'did not remain/was left', giving an impersonal general statement that sovereignty no longer exists around the world.
segments.s500-g222: همان adds emphasis ('that very/indeed'); 'بر زمین' is a locative 'upon the earth'; 'نامداری' is 'renown/notability'; again 'نماند' states that it 'did not remain/was left'. The clause parallels the previous one for rhetorical effect.
segments.s500-g223: که introduces an explanatory clause. سربسر ('entirely, wholly') + پیش من ('in my presence / before me') is an adverbial phrase modifying the predicate 'کهترند'; 'کهترند' is a present-tense plural predicate meaning 'they are inferior/lesser'. The negative نه (not) that appears in the line's sense governs the assertion that none are wholly inferior to the speaker.
segments.s500-g224: وگرچه is the concessive 'although/even though'; بلندند is a present plural predicate 'they are tall/high' (here conceding physical stature or prominence); و links to نیک‌اخترند, which describes them as 'well‑born/fortunate' (the adjective used as predicate). The whole clause concedes qualities while denying overall inferiority.
segments.s500-g209: چو آن پاسخ‌نامه شد is a temporal clause: چو (when) introduces the time, آن پاسَخ‌نامه (that reply/letter) is the subject, and شد is a past event-marker 'happened/was made'. Word order is topic–verb; the whole segment means 'when that reply came into being/was made'.
segments.s500-g210: زنی بود گویا به پیغمبری is the main clause following the temporal clause: زنی بود = 'there was a woman' (بود as past copula 'was'), گویا 'as if/it seemed' qualifies the whole clause, and به پیغمبری uses به + noun to indicate 'in the guise of / as a prophet' (a simile).
segments.s500-g211: ابا تاج و با جامه شاهوار presents the woman's attire. Here 'ابا' is an archaic/compounded spelling functioning like 'او با' ('she with'); تاج 'crown' and با جامه شاهوار 'with regal garments' are coordinated by و. The phrase is nominative/adjunct describing the subject.
segments.s500-g212: همی رفت با خوب‌رخ ده سوار is the action: همی رفت uses the present-imperfective particle همی (here continuous/poetic imperfect) + رفت (past stem) to give an ongoing narrative 'rode along'. با خوب‌رخ ده سوار is a prepositional phrase 'with ten fair-faced horsemen' (modifier of the verb).
segments.s500-g213: چو آمد خرامان به نزدیک شاه is another temporal/locative clause: چو آمد 'when she came', خرامان is an adverbial participle 'striding proudly/stately', and به نزدیک شاه 'near the king' gives the goal/location. Word order places the temporal marker first, then motion + manner + destination.
segments.s500-g214: پذیره فرستاد چندی به راه: فرستاد is the main past verb 'sent'; پذیره ('reception' / 'receiving party') is its object, so 'he sent a reception party'. چندی به راه is an adverbial 'some way out on the road' (چندی = for a short distance/for a while, به راه = onto the road/out to meet her).
segments.s500-g215: زن نامبردار نامه بداد: زن نامبردار 'the renowned/aforementioned woman' is the subject, نامه is the direct object 'the letter'. بداد here is an archaic or poetic verb form meaning 'gave/delivered' (formed from داد with prefix), so the clause means 'the renowned woman gave/delivered the letter.'
segments.s500-g216: پیام دلیران همه کرد یاد is a compact clause: پیام (the message) is the theme, دلیران همه 'all the brave men' is the affected group, and کرد یاد (literally 'made remembrance') is a verbal phrase meaning 'caused to remember / took note of' — so 'the message was noted by all the warriors.' Word order is object–agent–verb in poetic compression.
segments.s500-g241: فرستاده (subject) + آمد (simple past) is a straightforward noun + past-verb order: “the messenger came.” سخنها (plural object or adverbial phrase “words/utterances”) precedes the verb بگفت, where بگفت is a past-tense form (3rd person singular) with the archaic/poetic b- prefix; together سخنها بگفت = “he said/spoke words.”
segments.s500-g242: همه راز is the object phrase “all the secrets.” بیرون is an adverb meaning “out/away” placed before the verb, and کشید is the past tense 3rd-person singular of کشیدن “to pull/draw” (here: “drew out/revealed”). از نهفت is a prepositional phrase “from hiding” (از = “from”; نهفت = “that which is hidden”), supplying the source of what was drawn out.
segments.s500-g243: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones/elders.” یکی انجمن is an object phrase meaning “one (single) assembly” (numeric modifier one + noun). ساختند is the past-tense plural “they made/formed,” agreeing with the plural subject; word order is subject – object – verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s500-g244: ز گفتار introduces a source with the poetic preposition ز (= از) “from/of the saying.” دل را is the definite object “the heart” marked with را, and بپرداختند is the past-tense plural of پرداختن with the prefixed b- (they attended to / dealt with). The structure means they paid attention to or treated the utterance coming from the heart.
segments.s500-g245: که here introduces the reported clause (“that”). ما is the subject “we,” and برگزیدیم is the first-person plural past “we chose.” زن دو هزار is the direct object phrase “a woman of two thousand” (numeral follows the noun phrase) — the precise sense of “دو هزار” is numeric/quantifying here.
segments.s500-g246: A string of nominal predicates/appositions describing the woman: سخن‌گوی (“speaker/eloquent”), و (and), داننده (“knower/knowledgeable”), و, هوشیار (“alert/wise”). There is no copula; these nouns/adjectives postmodify زن and form a coordinated predicate.
segments.s500-g247: هر صدی is a quantified phrase “each/for every hundred.” بسته here functions as a past participle/adjectival verb meaning “bound/tied/fastened” and precedes the quantified gift phrase ده تاج زر (“ten gold crowns”), i.e. he attached or allotted ten gold crowns to each hundred. The exact role of the initial form ابا in the manuscript does not match the provided senses and appears irregular or corrupt here.
segments.s500-g248: بدو is the dative pronoun “to him/to her/to it.” در نشانده is a verbal phrase with نشانده (past participle/verb “placed/set”) and the locative در “in/into,” so together “set into.” فراوان گهر is the object “many jewels.” The sequence expresses that many gems were set into them (i.e. into the crowns), with the clitic-like dative (بدو) fronted.
segments.s500-g297: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. The clause uses a compressed classical sequence 'شب روز شد' (literally 'night became day') to mark the time, with شد as the change-of-state verb 'became'. اندرآمد is an older past form meaning 'entered' and the prepositional phrase به شهر (= 'into the city') indicates direction/place.
segments.s500-g298: به دیدار برداشت is an idiomatic sequence: به + دیدار ('for/to a visit') plus برداشت (past of برداشتن used here as 'set out/took up') meaning 'he set out to visit'. The following zān (زان) + شهر + بهر is an archaic word order: زان 'from/that', شهر 'the city', بهر 'for/toward'—together signaling 'from that city, for (the purpose of) visiting' or 'toward a visit from that city'.
segments.s500-g299: کم و بیش is the idiom 'more or less'. ایشان is the subject 'they'. همی is the archaic/prosaic continuous marker (common in epic/poetic Persian) signaling ongoing action. بازجست is a past tense of جستن 'sought/searched', so the whole clause means 'they more or less kept searching.'
segments.s500-g300: همی بود is 'it was/it continued' (همی again marks continuity). تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'. رازها شد درست uses شد as the change-of-state verb 'became' and درست as the predicative adjective 'clear/correct', so 'until the secrets became clear.'
segments.s500-g281: ببود is an archaic past-form meaning “there was” and here introduces the existence/state. The locative phrase اندر آن شهر (“in that city”) follows the verb, then the duration یک ماه (“one month”) and finally the nominative/subject شاه (“a king”) — a poetic (verb‑first) word order common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s500-g282: چو is the temporal conjunction “when”; آسوده گشتند is a compound past meaning “they became at ease/relieved” with گشتند in the past plural. The coordinated subject شاه و سپاه follows the verb (verb‑before‑subject order for poetic effect), and the plural verb agrees with the joined subjects.
segments.s500-g283: ازنجا is the adverb “from there.” بیامد is the simple past “came.” The following sequence دمان و دنان are treated as the coming parties (proper names or figures) and therefore function as the subject; because they are names/poetic terms they are not matched to the ordinary dictionary senses given.
segments.s500-g284: دل‌آراسته is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning “heart‑adorned” or “beautiful/charming,” modifying the coming. سوی is the preposition “toward,” and شهر زنان is a genitive phrase “the city of women” (شهر governed by the premodifier زنان). The phrase order places the descriptive adjective before the directional phrase.
segments.s500-g285: ز دریـا is the prepositional phrase “from the sea.” گذر کرد is the compound verb “passed” (گذر as noun/idea + کرد as light verb) and appears before its subject in this poetic order. زن دو هزار is the subject phrase meaning “two thousand women” (older/poetic Persian often puts the numeral after the noun).
segments.s500-g286: همه پاک qualifies the preceding women: همه “all” + پاک “pure/clean.” با introduces accompanying items “with” and links to the nouns افسر و گوشوار — here افسر is the poetic word for a helmet/diadem (not ‘officer’) and گوشوار means earrings/ornaments, indicating what they wore.
segments.s500-g287: یکی بیشه begins “a certain/one grove.” The sequence بد پر ز آب و درخت combines بد + پر (“…full of …”) + ز آب و درخت (“with water and trees”); بد here is archaic/poetic and does not mean a straightforward ‘bad’ — it functions as part of a compound phrase meaning something like “richly/abundantly full.” Word order places the fullness phrase after the head noun as descriptive material.
segments.s500-g288: همه جای is “every place/spot” (همه modifying جای). روشن‌دل is a compound adjective “bright‑hearted/cheerful” and نیکبخت follows with the coordinating و as “and fortunate/blessed.” Together they describe the grove’s pleasant/auspicious character.
segments.s500-g185: وگر is the conditional 'if'. مردوش here is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'a man' (literally ز + او contracted in similar passages; functionally 'he/a man'); باشد is the present-subjunctive/copula 'is' used in conditional clauses; و simply links 'man' and 'sarfraz' ('and'); سرفراز is an adjective 'proud/victorious' describing that man. Word order is typical Persian: conditional particle + subject phrase + copula/adjective.
segments.s500-g186: بسوی is the preposition 'toward'. هرومش (هروم) denotes a loud noise/uproar; فرستند is the past/pluperfect/poetic 3rd-plural form 'they send/they sent' (here used as a simple past/preterite action in narrative). باز is the noun 'hawk/falcon' (a common poetic image). The phrase literally 'they sent a hawk toward the uproar.'
segments.s500-g187: وگر 'if'. ز + و here represent the poetic contraction زُو = 'from him' (ز = 'from' + enclitic او 'him'); پسر زاید is 'a son is born' with زاید as the archaic/present form of 'beget/be born'. آنجا که هست is a relative clause 'there where he is' (که هست = 'that/where he is'). The line uses compact, archaic ordering typical of epic Persian.
segments.s500-g188: بباشد ... نباشد is a correlative pair meaning 'whether he be or be not' with both forms in the present-subjunctive/optative shape (archaic usage). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', ماش is the noun 'mung-bean' (literal or proverbial object), and دست is the noun 'hand' (here 'a hand on the mung-bean' — literal or idiomatic). Word order places the prepositional phrase before the noun دست as a unit.
segments.s500-g189: ز ما = 'from us'. هرک ('هرک') is an indefinite relative 'whoever/whichever of' (here 'whoever of us'). او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he' (referring back to that person). روزگار here means 'fate' or 'chance' and نبرد is used in the sense 'does not take away' (an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'carry off' or 'deprive'); together روزگار نبرد = 'if fate does not take (him)'. The clause is conditional: 'If fate does not carry off any of us...'.
segments.s500-g190: از اسپ اندر آرد is literally 'brings out from a horse/steed' with از + noun + اندر (poetic in/within) + آرد (poetic present/3rd sg of آورد 'bring'). یکی شیرمرد = 'one brave man' (یکی = one/a certain; شیرمرد = champion). The verb governs the following object phrase 'یکی شیرمرد'.
segments.s500-g191: یکی = 'one'/'a certain'. تاج زرینش = 'his golden crown' (زرین + possessive -ش). بر سر = prepositional phrase 'on (his) head'. نهیم is the 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative/imperative-tinged form of نهادن 'to place' (here 'let us place' / 'we shall place'), an archaic epic form. Overall: 'Let us place a golden crown on his head.'
segments.s500-g192: همان تخت او = 'that same throne of his' (همان = 'that/same', تخت = 'throne', او = possessive pronoun 'his' placed after the noun). بر دو پیکر = 'upon two pedestals/figures' (دو modifies پیکر); نهیم as above is 'we shall place/let us set'. The clause repeats the optative placing action for the throne.
segments.s500-g137: کسی کو ز فرمان ما سر بتافت is a compact relative clause: کسی کو = 'anyone who' (کسی + the archaic relative کو), ز فرمان ما = a prepositional phrase 'from/against our command', and سر بتافت is the verb phrase 'turned (his) head away' in the past (بتافت is 3rd sg. past with the archaic b- prefix common in epic Persian). Word order is subject/topic (کسی) + relative marker (کو) + prepositional phrase + verb at the end.
segments.s500-g138: نهالی به جز خاک تیره نیافت: نهالی ('a sapling / young shoot') is the grammatical subject, به جز ('except for') introduces the only thing found, خاک تیره = 'dark/bleak earth/soil' (adjective after noun, poetic order), and نیافت is the past negative 'did not find' (ن‑ + یافتن). The verb comes at the end as usual; the whole means that the sapling found nothing except dark earth.
segments.s500-g139: نخواهم که جایی بود در جهان: نخواهم is a negative volitional/present 'I do not wish / I will not have'; که introduces the subordinate clause; جایی ('a place/any place') is the indefinite subject and بود is the verb 'to be' (here functioning as the copula in the subordinate clause), followed by the locative در جهان. Classical Persian allows this order where the wish/volition governs the subordinate clause.
segments.s500-g140: که دیدار آن باشد از من نهان: that-clause continuing: دیدار آن = 'the sight/meeting of it' (دیدار used as 'seeing/sight'), باشد is the 3rd-sg. subjunctive/present of باش(یدن) used here in the subordinate clause ('should be / be'), and از من نهان = 'hidden from me' (از + agent/experiencer + predicate adjective نهان). The clause thus expresses the undesirable possibility the speaker rejects.
segments.s500-g141: گر آیم مرا با شما نیست رزم: گر آیم = 'if I come' (protasis). مرا را is the emphatic/dative object-form 'for me' (here segmented as م + را in the data), با شما = 'with you', and نیست رزم = 'there is no battle' (negation + predicate noun). Classical word order places the negated verb/particle before the predicate noun; the sense is 'if I come, there will be no fighting between me and you.'
segments.s500-g142: به دل آشتی دارم و رای بزم: به دل = 'in (my) heart', آشتی دارم = 'I have/feel reconciliation' (دارم present 'I have' used with a noun to express disposition), و joins the clauses, رای بزم is a compact noun phrase 'a plan/counsel for the feast' (رای modifies بزم); Persian often places modifier before the head in such condensed expressions.
segments.s500-g143: اگر هیچ دارید داننده‌ای: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'; هیچ here functions as an indefinite 'any/anyone' (classical usage) modifying دارید 'you have'; داننده‌ای is the indefinite noun 'a knower/expert' with the -ای suffix. The clause is elliptical and sets up a request or condition (see following segment).
segments.s500-g144: خردمند و بیدار خواننده‌ای: two adjectives precede the noun in classical order: خردمند 'wise', و 'and', بیدار 'alert/awake', modifying خواننده‌ای 'a reciter/reader' (the -ای ending again makes it indefinite or 'one who reads/recites'). This is an appositive describing the kind of person requested.
segments.s500-g193: همانا is an emphatic particle meaning 'verily/truly'. ز ما = 'from us' here means 'among us'; it precedes the noun phrase. زن بود is an existential/categorical statement in past tense (بود = 'was/were') with the noun 'زن' ('women') as predicate. سی‌هزار (the numeral) appears at the end to quantify the noun: 'there were thirty thousand women.'
segments.s500-g194: که is the relative/connector 'who/that' linking this descriptive clause to the women just mentioned. با + noun shows accompaniment/possession ('with' / 'wearing'); تاج is 'crown' and زرند functions adjectivally as 'golden' (poetic form). The second با introduces another accompanying ornament, گوشوار 'earring/ornament'. The verb 'to have/wear' is omitted—typical poetic compression.
segments.s500-g195: که here introduces the subordinate clause 'that/on which': مردی is an indefinite singular 'a man'; ز + گردنکشان = 'of/from the neck‑cutters' (a group name, i.e. rebels). روز جنگ is a temporal phrase 'on the day of battle' placed after the noun phrase as an adverbial.
segments.s500-g196: به here indicates direction/instrument ('into/by/with'); چنگال او = 'his claw' (lit. 'claw of him'). خاک شد is a compound verbal expression 'became dust' (شد = 'became' in past), used idiomatically for being destroyed/killed. بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay/instantly' modifying the whole verb phrase.
segments.s500-g197: تو is the singular emphatic 'you'. مردی بزرگی is a predicative nominal phrase: مردی ('a man') qualified by بزرگی ('greatness') = 'you are a great man'. و links this to the next clause. نامت is 'your name/reputation' with the possessive suffix -ت; بلند is an adjective here used figuratively as 'lofty/high' (fame).
segments.s500-g198: This is a negative imperative: مبند = 'do not bind/tie'. The prepositional phrase uses در نام ('in/with regard to your name/reputation') and بر خویشتن ('onto yourself'); classical Persian repeats prepositions for emphasis (در ... در). The sense is idiomatic: 'do not bind/attach yourself to your reputation.'
segments.s500-g199: که introduces reported speech/explanation 'for/that'. گویند is an impersonal present 'they say' (reportative). با زن = 'with a woman'; برآویختنی is a deverbal adjective/participle from برآویختن meaning 'apt to grapple/attack'—here describing the subject as one who grapples with women. The construction reports reputation/rumor.
segments.s500-g200: ز is the preposition 'from/of'. آویختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to cling/attach oneself' (here meaning 'clinging/holding on'); نیز 'also/even' adds emphasis; بگریختی is past 2nd‑person singular 'you fled/ran away'. Together: 'you even fled from clinging.'
segments.s500-g41: بجوییم is the 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive formed with the prefix بـ (’let us…’) from جست/جوی meaning “seek/search.” و simply links the two verbs (“and”). بر کوه خارا بریم is a verbless directional clause where بر + noun gives the goal/position (“onto/to the mountain”), خارا describes the mountain (’flinty/rocky’), and بریم is the 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive ‘let us go/let us proceed’ (poetic form equivalent to برویم).
segments.s500-g42: پر اندیشه و پر مدارا بریم is a parallel descriptive clause: پر here means “full (of)” and takes the following noun (اندیشه = thought, مدارا = tolerance/forbearance). The repeated بریم (same verb form as above) returns the clause to the verb ‘let us go/let us proceed,’ so the whole line means ‘let us go, full of thought and full of forbearance.’
segments.s500-g43: بدان is a fused form (ب + دان) functioning as an imperative/particle meaning roughly ‘know this’ or ‘let it be so’; تا introduces a purpose/temporal clause ‘until/so that,’ and نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive meaning ‘he should not come / does not come.’ بدین روی کوه is a frozen locution meaning ‘to this face/side of the mountain’ (بدین = ‘in this manner/on this side’, روی = side/face, کوه = mountain) giving the place clause.
segments.s500-g44: نینجامید is an archaic/poetic negative perfect/past meaning ‘was not accomplished / did not come about.’ از ما marks the source/possessor ‘from us / of ours.’ گروها گروه is a rhythmic reduplication meaning ‘bands upon bands / group after group’ or simply ‘our bands,’ where گروها is the plural ‘bands’ and گروه reiterates/collects the notion of a company.
segments.s500-g45: بفرمود is the polite/honorific past of گفتن/فرمودن ‘he ordered/said.’ سالار is a title ‘chief/commander,’ دیهیم is an adjective meaning ‘rural/village (of the village),’ and جوی here is a noun ‘search’ (object of the order): together ‘the village chief ordered a search.’
segments.s500-g46: که introduces reported speech (‘that’). آن روز = ‘that day.’ ندهند is the negative present/subjunctive/plural ‘they should not give / they must not give.’ چیز is the general noun ‘thing/anything,’ and بدوی (here) means ‘original/at first’ or idiomatically ‘anything at all’ — together the clause reports the chief’s order that nothing should be given on that day.
segments.s500-g47: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when.’ گاه خورش = ‘the time of the food/meal’ (گاه = time/occasion, خورش = cooked dish/meal). درگذشت is the past tense ‘passed/was over.’ The noun اژدها follows (poetic inversion) as the subject: ‘when the mealtime had passed, the dragon…’.
segments.s500-g48: بیامد is the simple past ‘came/appeared.’ چو here is a simile ‘like/as.’ آتش + بران functions as a compound image ‘burning fire’ (آتش = fire; بران in poetry often forms a participial/relational sense ‘blazing/that which burns’), تند is an adverb/adjective ‘swift/fierce,’ and جا = ‘place/spot’ — together: ‘came like blazing fire, swift to the place.’
segments.s500-g33: یکی is an indefinite determiner meaning “a certain / one,” placed before the noun. اژدهایست is a fused poetic form (اژدها + ست[=است]) meaning “is a dragon” (3rd-person copula attached). زان روی کوه is a locative phrase: زان = “that / from that,” روی = “side / face / on,” and کوه = “mountain,” so together “on that side of the mountain.”
segments.s500-g34: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause (“so that / which / who”). مرغ آید is a simple present/poetic present (‘a bird comes’) with آید from آمدن. از marks cause/agent here (‘from / because of’), رنج = “suffering / pain,” زهرش = “its poison” (زهر + enclitic ش), and ستوه is a predicative adjective meaning “weary / sick of,” completing the clause: ‘a bird that comes is weary from the suffering of its poison.’
segments.s500-g35: نیارد is a negative/denying form in the line’s diction (here meaning something like ‘will not permit / does not let’); this is an older/poetic verbal form rather than the noun-senses listed for the lemma. گذشتن is the infinitive ‘to pass,’ and بروبر is an adverb meaning ‘straight/face-to-face / directly,’ modifying گذشتن. سپاه is the noun ‘host / army’ that is prevented from passing.
segments.s500-g36: همی is an adverb meaning ‘continually / always’ (here marking ongoing action). دود is the subject ‘smoke,’ زهرش is the genitive ‘its poison,’ and برآید is the poetic present/3rd-person verb ‘rises / ascends.’ به + ماه gives the goal/extent ‘up to the moon / toward the moon.’
segments.s500-g37: همی is an adverb meaning ‘continually / always.’ آتش افروزد is a 3rd-person poetic present (افروزد = ‘kindles / kindled’) with آتش as object. از introduces the source/cause ‘from,’ کام = ‘desire / wish,’ and اوی is an older enclitic meaning ‘his / its,’ so the clause reads ‘he continually kindles fire from his desire.’
segments.s500-g38: دو گیسو بود is a nominal clause ‘there were two locks/tresses of hair’ with بود as the past copula. پیل را دام اوی places the target with the object-marker را: ‘(for) the elephant (پیل را) [they were] his trap (دام اوی).’ The enclitic اوی on دام marks possession (‘his trap’).
segments.s500-g39: همه شهر is the whole city (subject/topic). با او here reads ‘with him / alongside him’ or ‘compared to him,’ and نداریم is 1st-person-plural present ‘we do not have / there is not’ (negation + have). تاو is a noun meaning ‘radiance / glow,’ placed after the verb as the thing not possessed: ‘the whole city has not his radiance’ (i.e., none in the city equals his brilliance).
segments.s500-g40: خورش is ‘food / cooked dish’ (here meaning what must be fed). بایدش is a modal construction ‘it is necessary for him / he needs’ (باید + enclitic ش = ‘for him’), so together ‘his food requires / he must have.’ هر شبی means ‘each night’ (هر = every + شبی ‘night’), and پنج گاو is the quantity ‘five cows,’ the thing required nightly.
segments.s500-g97: Literal order: numeric determiner + adjective + noun + locative phrase. یکی (a certain/one) introduces an indefinite single person; مرده (adjective) precedes the noun مرد (man) — a common poetic order meaning “a dead man.” اندر(ان) is an archaic locative/partitive meaning “among them/therein,” and تخت‌بر (throne‑bearer) labels the group he belongs to (appositive/genitive notion).
segments.s500-g98: همانا is a discourse particle meaning “verily/indeed.” که introduces the following clause. بودش is the past form of بودن with the enclitic -ش (=“his/its”), here meaning “was his / existed for him”; پس از is the compound preposition “after,” with از functioning as the ‘from/after’ element, and مرگ فر = “death (مرگ) of farr (فر)” — together: “his farr was (still) after death.” The clause uses past tense and an elevated, slightly inverted word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s500-g99: ز + noun gives the material/source: ز دیبا “from (of) brocade.” کشیده is the past participle “drawn/strewn,” modifying چادری (a chador/veil). برو here is a compact poetic form of بر او (“upon him”); the phrase therefore means “a chador of brocade drawn over him.” Word order is compact and somewhat inverted for metrical reasons.
segments.s500-g100: ز هر گوهری = “of every jewel” (material/source), بر سرش = “upon his head” (بر + سر + enclitic ش = his). افسری is a noun meaning a diadem/ornamental headpiece. The phrase places the diadem (افسری) on his head and describes it as made/from every kind of jewel.
segments.s500-g101: همه گرد بر گرد او uses repeated گرد (“around”) for poetic emphasis: همه (all/everything) + گرد (around) + بر گرد او (about him). سیم و زر is a simple noun phrase “silver and gold” serving as the material/what was all around him. The order emphasizes the surrounding wealth.
segments.s500-g102: کسی را نبودی is a negative existential: کسی (anyone/no one in negative context) + را (definite/emphatic marker) + نبودی (past negative of بودن, 'was not / there was not'). بروبر is an adverbial compound meaning “face to face / close at hand,” and گذر means “passing/passage.” Together: “there was no one who passed by closely.”
segments.s500-g103: هرآنکس که is the indefinite relative “whoever/whosoever.” رفتی here functions as a past/action verb “went” (classical/poetic past used for a generic person), and بران کوهسار = بر + آن + کوهسار (“to/on that mountainside”) — بران is a contracted/poetic form meaning ‘to/on that.’ The clause sets up an agent who goes to that place.
segments.s500-g104: که introduces the subordinate clause “who/that.” از marks source/target (“from the dead”), مرده is the noun “the dead/corpse(s),” چیزی is “something,” کند is the present/subjunctive/optative form of کردن used here as the verb “does / would do / seeks to do,” and خواستار is an adjectival/predicative form meaning “desirous/seeking.” The phrase means “who seeks (or would seek) something from the dead,” with poetic verb placement (چیزی کند خواستار ≈ “is one who would do/seeks something”).
segments.s500-g105: بران کوه is a locative phrase (بران = 'on/at that', کوه = 'mountain') placed before the predication for emphasis. از بیم (از + بیم) gives the cause: 'from/because of fear.' لرزان is a predicative adjective 'trembling' and شدی is the past 2nd-person singular of شدن ('you became'), so together از بیم لرزان شدی = 'you became trembling from fear.'
segments.s500-g106: به مردی here marks the person toward/at whom the fear is directed ('in the presence of/at the sight of a man'). و simply connects the two clauses. بر جای is an adverbial phrase 'on the spot' and ریزان is a predicative adjective meaning 'scattered/falling apart'; شدی again is past 2nd-person singular 'you became,' so بر جای ریزان شدی = 'you became scattered on the spot' (i.e. fell into disorder).
segments.s500-g107: سکندر is the subject. برآمد is the past tense verb 'rose/came up/ascended.' بران کوه‌سر is an adverbial phrase 'onto that mountain-summit' (بران = 'that, thereon', کوه‌سر = 'mountain ridge/summit'), placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s500-g108: نظاره here functions as a verbal (averbal noun used as the action 'looked/observed') governed by سکندر earlier. بران مرد = 'at that man' (بر = preposition + ان demonstrative), and با سیم و زر is a modifying phrase 'with silver and gold' describing the man. The clause means 'he observed that man, who was adorned with silver and gold.'
segments.s500-g109: یکی is an indefinite 'someone/a certain (one).' بانگ بشنید is a simple past clause 'heard a cry' (بشنید = 3rd-person past of شنیدن). کای شهریار is a vocative: کای is the old poetic vocative 'O' (often used before royal titles) and شهریار 'king,' so the line reports that someone heard an exclamation addressed to the king.
segments.s500-g110: بسی here is an intensive/adverbial 'much/many.' بردی is 2nd-person past ('you took/won/did') used poetically for 'you have accomplished/you have carried off.' اندر جهان روزگار is a circumstantial phrase 'in the world / in the course of time' (اندر = 'in', جهان = 'world', روزگار = 'time/fate'). Together: 'You have done much in the world.'
segments.s500-g111: بسی is again 'many/much.' تخت شاهان is a genitive phrase 'the thrones of kings' (تخت = throne, شاهان = kings). بپرداختی is past 2nd-person singular of پرداختن; in this poetic context it means 'you dealt with/overthrew/ruined' (i.e. handled many kings' thrones), so the clause says 'you have dealt with/overthrown many kings' thrones.'
segments.s500-g112: سرت را = 'your head' with the object marker را. به گردون is a directional phrase 'to/toward the sky/heaven' (گردون = sky/heaven, also 'fate' in other contexts). برافراختی is past 2nd-person singular 'you raised/erected,' so سرت را به گردون برافراختی = 'you raised your head to the heavens' (i.e. became arrogant/proud).
segments.s500-g153: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." دانا functions as a noun phrase "the wise man/sage." به نزدیک ایشان is a prepositional phrase (به + نزدیک + ایشان) meaning "toward/near them," placed before the verb as is normal in Persian; رسید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular "arrived/approached." Together they form a subordinate temporal clause: "When the wise man came near them..."
segments.s500-g154: همه شهر is a noun phrase "the whole/entire city." زن دید is a past tense clause where زن is the object (here used collectively, "women") and دید is the past of دیدن "he saw; beheld." و links the two coordinated verbs/clauses; مردی ندید is the negative past (ندید = did not see) with مردی "a man" (indefinite) as its object: "he saw the entire city (to be) women and saw no man."
segments.s500-g155: همه لشکر is the subject "the whole army/host." از شهر بیرون is a compound locative phrase (از = "out of/from," شهر = "the city," بیرون = "out/outside") describing motion out of the city. شدند is the past 3rd‑person plural of شدن; here it indicates that they put themselves in that state of being out/they departed (classical Persian often uses شدن as the intransitive past).
segments.s500-g156: به دیدار رومی is a goal/目的 phrase: به + دیدار ("to a meeting") + رومی (object of the meeting, "the Roman/Byzantine"). به هامون شد(ند) places them on the plain: به + هامون = "to the plain." شدند again marks the past plural result/location: "they went/arrived to meet the Romans on the plain."
segments.s500-g157: بران is an adverbial "thereupon/on that account." نامه‌بر (literally "letter‑carrier") is the noun subject/actor; شد is the past of شدن used here to mean "was made/sent" (a common way to express that someone was appointed or a messenger dispatched). جهان انجمن is a compound nominal phrase "the world's assembly" or "the universal assembly/meeting," placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s500-g158: از ایشان = "from among them" (از + ایشان). هرانکس که introduces a relative clause "whoever (such that) ..." که is the relativizer "who/that." بد رای زن is a nominal phrase meaning literally "bad counsel/opinion regarding the woman" (بد = bad, رای = counsel/opinion, زن = woman). The clause is elliptical (no finite verb) and modifies هرانکس, i.e. "from among them, whoever had bad counsel about the woman..."
segments.s500-g159: چو again is the temporal conjunction "when." این نامه is the direct object "this letter." برخواند is a compound verb meaning "read aloud/recited" (بر + خواندن used in the sense of reading aloud or announcing). دانای شهر (lit. "wise of the city") is a genitive noun phrase meaning "the city's wise man/sage" and here is the subject of برخواند (inverted word order: verb before subject).
segments.s500-g160: ز رای دل شاه is a genitive/partitive cluster: ز (poetic for از) = "from/of," رای = "counsel/opinion," دل شاه = "the king's heart" (i.e. heartfelt counsel). برداشت is the past of برداشتن "took up/accepted" (here "adopted/took to heart"). بهر is a short prepositional particle "for/toward" (often marking purpose) and here signals orientation or purpose connected to برداشت; the clause thus means "he took up the king's heartfelt counsel (for [the intended purpose])."
segments.s500-g113: بسی (= much/enough) modifies the coordinated noun phrase 'دشمن و دوست' ('enemies and friends'). The verb کردی is 2nd-person singular past ('you did/made') and takes the preceding noun phrase as its object; تباه is a predicative adjective meaning 'ruined' that completes the verb phrase (you 'made ... ruined'). Word order (object before verb) is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s500-g114: ز گیتی = 'from/with regard to the world' (poetic prepositional phrase). کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. بازگشتست is a literary/poetic third‑person singular verbal form meaning 'there is a return' / 'the return has occurred' (stative/resultative sense). گاه is the noun 'time' and here is the logical subject/predicate complement: 'now the time of returning from the world has come.'
segments.s500-g115: رخ شاه is a possessive juxtaposition ('the king's face'). ز + آواز here is causal ('because of the sound/voice'). شد is past ('became'), and چون + چراغ is a simile: 'became like a lamp.' The clause describes a change of appearance caused by a sound.
segments.s500-g116: ازان کوه برگشت is a locative/source phrase + intransitive verb: 'from that mountain returned/came back.' دل is core noun 'heart' and پر ز داغ is a predicate phrase 'full of grief' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = 'of/from'; داغ = 'grief/burning sorrow'). The verb precedes the subject phrase ('برگشت دل'), a flexible poetic order.
segments.s500-g117: همی is an archaic/progressive particle used with past verbs to indicate repeated or continuous action in narrative ('kept on / was continually'). رفت is simple past ('went'). با نامداران is an instrumental/associative phrase ('with renowned men'), and روم is the destination ('to Rome') used without an overt preposition in this poetic line.
segments.s500-g118: بدان is the prepositional/demonstrative combination ('to/into that') introducing the place phrase, followed by شارستان 'city/town'. شد here functions as a light verb meaning 'there came/there happened' (it became that). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...', and خوانی هروم together describe the resulting noise: خوانی = a calling/cry, هروم = uproar/commotion. The clause therefore says that into/at that city an uproar of cries arose.
segments.s500-g119: که begins a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'). آن شهر = 'that city'. یکسر 'entirely' modifies زنان 'women'; داشتند is past plural ('they had/possessed' — here 'was inhabited by' or 'contained'), so the clause means 'that city was entirely of women.'
segments.s500-g120: کسی را functions as the (definite/emphatic) object; in a negative context with نگذاشتند ('they did not allow'), کسی را means 'no one' (i.e. nobody was permitted). دران شهر 'in that city' is the locative, and نگذاشتند is past negative of گذاشتن used here to mean 'allow/permit' — 'they did not let anyone into that city.'
segments.s500-g257: فرستاده (subject) + برگشت (simple past) gives 'the messenger returned'. The coordinating و links a second clause whose object پاسخ precedes the past verb بگفت (classical past tense 'he said' / 'he spoke'), so the whole clause reads 'and (he) gave an answer.' Note the literary past بَ- prefix in بگفت.
segments.s500-g258: سخنها (plural 'words/speeches') + همه ('all') is the topic phrase; با خرد is a prepositional phrase 'with wisdom'. بود is the past copula 'were'. جفت is used predicatively/qualifyingly (literary) to mean 'matched' or 'paired/fitting' — so 'all the words were matched with wisdom.'
segments.s500-g259: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. The preposition ز ('from', poetic) introduces منزل ('station/camp'), followed by the object سپه 'host/army' and the past verb برگرفت 'took up/raised/mustered' — literal word order: 'Alexander from the station the host raised.'
segments.s500-g260: ز marks the cause/agent 'by/at'; کار here means 'deeds/affairs', and زنان is 'women'. مانده is a stative past 'remained/was left' (literary), and اندر شگفت is a locative phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' — altogether 'he remained in wonder at the deeds of the women.'
segments.s500-g261: دو منزل بیامد is a temporal/sequence phrase 'he came two stages' (i.e., after two stages). یکی introduces an incident 'once' or 'one (time)', باد خاست is literary past 'a wind arose' (خاست = 'arose').
segments.s500-g262: وزو is a compressed/poetic form (here functioning like 'from it' / 'from that') followed by برف 'snow'. با + کوه 'with mountain(s)' links the accompaniment. درگشت راست is a compact classical phrase: درگشت used as a noun/verb 'turning/way' and راست 'straight/right' — together indicating the route/conditions turned direct/right; word order is typical of older Persian where particles and adjectives follow nouns.
segments.s500-g263: تبه شد is a literary/perfective construction meaning 'were ruined/destroyed' (تبه here 'ruin/mischief' intransitively). بسی مردم پایکار places the affected group after an adverbial 'many (basi) people of the contest/combat' — i.e. 'many warriors/people engaged in battle.'
segments.s500-g264: ز here marks cause 'because of / from' and is followed by the conjoined causes سرما و برف 'cold and snow'. اندر آن روزگار is a temporal locative 'in that time/season' that explains when/under what circumstances this happened.
segments.s500-g49: سکندر (proper name) + بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb used here as 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/command) clause — تا + verb will take the subjunctive in the following clause. لشکرش is لشکر (army) with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his'.
segments.s500-g50: یکی here functions adverbially ('at once' / 'together') modifying the action; تیرباران is a nominal compound meaning 'a shower/volley of arrows' and functions as the object; کنند is the present-subjunctive form (they do) used after تا in a commanded/prospective clause; ازبرش is opaque here (not the common senses of 'by heart' given in the lemma list) and likely a compressed/poetic locative/partitive form — sense assignment is uncertain.
segments.s500-g51: بزد is simple past (he struck/struck down); یک دم is a temporal phrase 'one moment / in a single instant' (دم = moment here); آن is the demonstrative 'that' pointing to the creature; اژدهای is the genitival form 'dragon' (اژدَه + -ی); پلید is an attributive adjective 'vile/filthy' modifying 'dragon'. Word order: verb before its temporal phrase and the noun phrase follows, as common in poetic older Persian.
segments.s500-g52: تنی چند is an idiom meaning 'several (bodies/men)' (تنی here = 'a certain/some'); ازیشان = از + ایشان 'from/of them' (partitive/obj source); به + دم here means 'by the tail' with دم used as 'tip/edge/tail' (sense 'tail'); درکشید is past 'drew in / pulled' (subject continuity implies the dragon performed this action). The sequence shows how the dragon immediately seized a few persons 'by the tail' and dragged them.
segments.s500-g53: بفرمود again is the honorific past used as 'he ordered/commanded.' Here it governs the following name: اسکندر (alternate spelling of Alexander) is the subject, and فیلقوس is a proper name in the object/indirect-object position (the addressee of the order). The sequence is terse/poetic: 'Alexander ordered Phylacus.'
segments.s500-g54: تبیره is uncertain in the provided sense list (the lemma senses given are 'interpretation/explanation' which do not fit the immediate context); به is the preposition 'to/for' marking the beneficiary/direction; زخم is 'wound' (here 'to the wounded' or 'for the wounds'); آوریدند is past plural 'they brought'; و is the coordinating 'and'; کوس is 'war-drum' (an instrument used as signal). Grammatically, this segment says that torches/drums were brought to/for the wounded after the command.
segments.s500-g55: همان is a demonstrative 'that' (or 'the same'), pointing back to what was brought/arranged; بی‌کران is an adjective 'boundless/endless' modifying آتش 'fire'; افروختند is past plural 'they kindled' (causative/active). The clause reports the result of the preparations — they lit a huge fire.
segments.s500-g56: به هرجای is a locative phrase 'to/every place' (به + هرجای 'wherever/everywhere'); مشعل 'torch' is the subject/object depending on reading but here refers to torches that were burning; همی is an older/poetic ongoing marker meaning 'still/kept on' or stresses continuous action; سوختند is past plural 'they burned' (i.e., the torches burned). Together: 'torches burned everywhere.'
segments.s500-g145: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when.” برخواند is a past tense verb formed from خواندن with the prefix بر- meaning “read aloud/recited” (3rd singular past). این نامه پندمند is the object phrase: این (demonstrative) + نامه (noun 'letter') modified postpositively by the adjective پندمند ('admonitory/wise') — classical Persian often places adjectives after the noun.
segments.s500-g146: برآنکس is a deictic/locative phrase meaning roughly “toward that person” (بر- marking direction/target). که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” هست is the copula ‘is’ in present form. از شما marks source/agent in the sense 'by you' (classical use), and ارجمند is the predicate/adjective ‘esteemed’ applying to آنکس.
segments.s500-g147: ببندید is a 2nd-person plural/formal imperative of بستن meaning 'close/shut.' پیش آمدن is a deverbal noun phrase 'coming forward/approach' (پیش giving the sense of 'forward/in front'), and را is the definite-object marker attaching to that verbal noun. میان is a locative adverb/preposition 'between' indicating where to close the approach.
segments.s500-g148: کزین is a fused preposition meaning 'from this.' آمدن is the verbal noun 'coming/arrival.' کس in the negative context functions as 'anyone/no one,' and ندارد is the present (or stative) verb 'has/does not have' — here yielding 'no one has harm.' زیان is the noun 'harm/loss.' Together the clause means 'from this coming no one will have harm' (i.e. no one will be harmed).
segments.s500-g149: بفرمود is a past/honorific verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (classical register). تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here meaning 'that/so that.' فیلسوفی appears as an indefinite noun 'a philosopher' (the -ی ending makes it indefinite), and ز روم is a prepositional phrase 'from Rome' with poetic ز for از.
segments.s500-g150: برد is the past form of بردن 'to take/bring' (here 'brought/bear/bring'); the provided sense entries do not list that verbal sense, so it is marked unknown. نامه is the direct object 'the letter.' نزدیک شهر is a locative phrase 'near the city' (نزدیک as prepositional/adverbial), and هروم follows as a descriptive epithet (either a place-name or 'uproar'), giving 'the city of uproar' or 'the city Herom.'
segments.s500-g151: بسی here functions adverbially as 'many/very' (classical usage), and نیز means 'also.' شیرین modifies سخنها, so شیرین سخنها is 'pleasant/sweet speeches/words' (سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches'). بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said/spoke.'
segments.s500-g152: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger/envoy' and خود is a possessive/emphatic 'his own.' با خرد بود جفت is a predicative phrase: با خرد 'with wisdom' and بود (copula 'was') linking to جفت used adjectivally 'paired/matched' — meaning the messenger was matched with (i.e. endowed with or accompanied by) wisdom.
segments.s500-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. نزدیکی نرم‌پایان is a noun phrase meaning 'the nearness of the light-footed (ones)'; نرم‌پایان (light-footed) modifies نزدیکی (nearness) in a poetic, compact construction. رسید is a simple past verb 'arrived/drew near', so the whole segment means 'when the light-footed came near'.
segments.s500-g2: نگه کرد is a compound verbal phrase: noun نگه ('gaze, pause') + past auxiliary کرد, giving 'he looked/paused'. و coordinates two actions. مردم بی‌اندازه دید follows Persian SOV order (noun phrase before verb): مردم 'people', بی‌اندازه an adverbial quantifier 'exceedingly/innumerable', and دید 'he saw' (past). Together: 'he paused and saw countless people.'
segments.s500-g3: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...' listing items without an explicit verb (a common poetic verbless enumeration). The items are simple nouns: اسپ 'steed', جوشن 'cuirass/armor', تیغ 'sword', گرز 'mace'.
segments.s500-g4: ازان 'from/of those' introduces a partitive reference to the previously mentioned troops. هر یکی 'each one' (literally 'every one'). چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. یکی سرو برز is a compact simile: یکی 'a single/one', سرو 'cypress', برز an archaic adjective 'high/lofty' placed after the noun; together 'each one like a single lofty cypress.'
segments.s500-g5: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. رعد خروشان is a noun plus participial adjective 'roaring thunder' (noun before adjective in Persian poetry). برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth' and غریو is the object 'a cry/shout'; word order is poetically inverted but reads 'when the roaring thunder issued forth a cry.'
segments.s500-g6: برهنه سپاهی is an adjective + noun phrase 'naked/bare warriors' (adjective preceding noun). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/character of' (به used adverbially here), and دیو is the simile 'demon'. So the phrase means 'warriors bare, in behavior like demons.'
segments.s500-g7: یکی here functions adverbially/collectively (a terse poetic marker, 'one/at once' or 'some'), سنگ‌باران (stone-shower) + بکردند (they did) forms a verbal compound meaning 'they showered with stones / they stoned'. سخت is an adverb meaning 'severely/fiercely', modifying the action.
segments.s500-g8: چو here is a simile 'like/as'. باد خزان 'the autumn wind' (noun + attributive noun). برزند is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'they strike/fall upon' or 'they beat' (third-person plural), and بر درخت is the prepositional target 'upon the tree'. The whole simile compares the stoning to the way an autumn wind lashes a tree.
segments.s500-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از گاو is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the cow' placed before the noun that follows; پیوندش is پیوند + enclitic -ش 'its', here meaning 'its connection/attachment'; آگنده شد is an adjective آگنده 'filled/saturated' with the past auxiliary شد, forming a predicative clause 'became filled/attached'. The whole segment reads 'when its connection to the cow became (filled/established)'.
segments.s500-g82: بر اندام is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over the body' (بر + اندام). زهرش is زهر 'poison' with the possessive -ش 'its/ his', and پراگنده شد is the adjective پراگنده 'scattered' with شد, meaning 'was scattered/spread'. Together: 'its poison was scattered over its body.'
segments.s500-g83: همه modifies رودگانیش (رودگان + possessive -ش) = 'all its rivers/streams' — in context a poetic term for veins or channels. سوراخ کرد is a compound where سوراخ (hole) + کرد 'made/caused' yields 'pierced/made holes in'. The order (object before verb) is typical poetic Persian.
segments.s500-g84: به مغز و به پی راه are two prepositional phrases introduced by به 'into/to': به مغز 'into the brain' and به پی راه 'into/along the پی (track/foot) of the way' — together indicating direction/target. گستاخ کرد has گستاخ 'audacious/bold' used with کرد 'made' to mean 'forcibly/boldly made/forced (a way)'. The clause describes forcing a bold passage toward these inner parts.
segments.s500-g85: همی is a habitual/continuous marker ('kept on, continuously'); زد is the past tense 'struck'; سرش را combines سر 'head' + possessive ش 'its/his' and the object marker را, so 'his/its head (as direct object)'; بران is an older/prepositional form 'on that' and precedes کوه سنگ 'rocky mountain'. The phrase means 'kept striking his/its head on that rocky mountain.'
segments.s500-g86: چنین 'thus/so' gives the manner; تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until'; برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'; زمانی here functions adverbially 'for a while/after a time'; درنگ is the noun 'pause/delay' serving as the subject of 'برآمد'. The clause says 'thus until a pause arose after a while.'
segments.s500-g87: سپاهی is the subject 'the army/soldiers'. بروبر is an adverb meaning 'directly/face-to-face' or 'right upon'. ببارید is the past of باریدن 'to fall/rain' used metaphorically with تیر 'arrows' to mean 'showered/fell (arrows)'. The poetic order places the falling-thing تیر after the verb.
segments.s500-g88: به پای آمد is a verbal phrase 'came to the foot/arrived at the base' (به + پای + آمد). آن کوه نخچیرگیر is the subject phrase 'that mountain (نخچیرگیر is an epithet 'hunter-catcher' or 'that which snags game') following the verb — a poetic word order. Together it says 'that (hunter-catching) mountain came to the foot/arrived at the base.'
segments.s500-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. کوه is the subject; از تبیره is an ablative phrase 'from/with (the) tabireh' giving the cause or source of the sound; پرآواز is a predicative adjective 'full of sound / loud-voiced' and گشت is the past-tense verb 'became'. Word order is subject + oblique phrase + predicate, typical of epic poetry.
segments.s500-g58: بترسید is a past tense verb meaning 'they feared / were terrified' (3rd-person plural implied); ازان اژدها is a prepositional phrase 'of/from that dragon' marking the cause of fear; بازگشت is a second past verb 'returned / turned back' coordinated with the first verb by simple juxtaposition (no conjunction), a common poetic device.
segments.s500-g59: چو = 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد is an archaic verbal form meaning 'appeared/rose' or 'came forth' here functioning with سر ('head') as a light phrase 'lifted/raised its head'; از برج گاو is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the Tower of the Bull' (برج used as the zodiac sign). The clause follows an elevated, slightly inverted poetic word order (subject + archaic verb + complement + locative).
segments.s500-g60: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گلزاربرخاست is a fused verbal phrase 'rose from the flower-garden' (subject/phrase expressed compactly). بانگ is 'the sound/cry' and چکاو is the bird 'lark' (so 'the cry/voice of the lark'). The phrase places the verb before its nominal subject/possessive phrase in a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s500-g61: چو = 'when'. آن اژدها را uses را as the definite/object marker, presenting 'that dragon' as the definite patient/affected entity; خورش is a noun 'a stew / the meal' and بود is the past copula 'was' i.e. 'had (a) meal' — together meaning 'when that dragon had its meal' (گاه 'occasion' emphasizes timing). The clause is an adverbial temporal clause setting the time for the following action.
segments.s500-g62: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. مردان لشکر is a genitival phrase 'the men of the host/army'. گزین کرد is a compound past 'chose/selected' (گزین + کرد), and شاه 'king' appears post-verbally as subject, a normal rhetorical inversion in epic verse (verb before subject). The whole means 'the king chose from the men of the army.'
segments.s500-g63: درم is the object 'dirhams' (coins); داد is the past verb 'gave'; سالار 'the commander' appears after the verb as subject (poetic inversion). چندی ز گنج is an adverbial phrase 'some (amount) from the treasure' specifying source and quantity. Overall: 'the commander gave some dirhams from the treasury.'
segments.s500-g64: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought' (3rd person). با خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'with himself / with him' indicating accompaniment. گاو (followed by the numeral پنج in the line) is the noun 'cow' and appears before the numeral (a common poetic order), so the clause means 'he brought with him five cows.'
segments.s500-g265: برآمد is a past/imperfective verb meaning 'arose' or 'issued forth' placed before its nominal phrase for poetic effect. یکی here means 'a certain' (indefinite determiner) and precedes the nouns it qualifies. ابر و دودی سیاه is a coordination: ابر (cloud) and دودی (a wisp/thing of smoke) with the adjective سیاه (black) following and modifying دودی in the usual Persian order (noun + adjective).
segments.s500-g266: بر is the preposition 'over/on/against' governing آتش (the fire). همی رفت is an older/poetic progressive/imperfective: همی + past-stem رفت gives a sense of 'was going/kept going' (continuous motion). گفتی here is an archaic/formal simile-marker (cf. گویی) introducing a comparison, and سپاه is the noun 'army' that completes that comparison ('as if an army').
segments.s500-g267: زره کتف آزادگان را is a definite noun phrase with the object marker را attached (marking the armor as a definite object). Grammatically the verb بسوخت follows and denotes burning: with را present the clause reads as a (transitive) action affecting that armor (in poetic context the agent may be implicit). The genitive relationship 'کتف آزادگان' is 'the shoulder (portion) of the free/men of rank.'
segments.s500-g268: ز is the poetic preposition meaning 'from' or 'because of' and governs نعل سواران ('the horseshoes of the riders' — نعل governing سواران in a possessive/genitive relation). زمین is the subject/affected locus and برفروخت is a compound-like past verb here meaning 'was set afire' or 'kindled' (poetic lexical choice rather than the commercial sense 'sold').
segments.s500-g269: بدین here is the fused prepositional expression (به + این) meaning 'by/with this'; هم نشان emphasizes 'this very sign' or 'also this sign'. تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until' that connects to the following clause; به شهری رسید is a normal Persian prepositional phrase + past verb where به شهری = 'to a city' and رسید = 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s500-g270: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('where/that'), مردم is the clause's subject 'the people', and بسان is the comparative preposition 'like/as'. شب تیره is a noun (شب) modified by the adjective تیره (dark/bleak) — the whole clause with the final verb دید (past of دیدن) functions as 'were seen/appeared' (i.e., 'the people were like a dark night').
segments.s500-g271: فروهشته is a past/passive participial or adjectival form meaning 'scattered' or 'cast down' and precedes the noun it qualifies in this poetic ordering. لفچ and کفچ are diminutive/obscure items (small wrappers and little shoes); their coordination with و links two affected items. برآورده here functions as a participial/adjectival phrase meaning 'raised/hoisted/piled up' describing کفچ (poetic verb placing the state after the noun).
segments.s500-g272: به کردار is a idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in the manner/appearance of'; قیر is the simile's ground ('tar, pitch'). شبه before کفچ و لفچ functions as 'resembling' or 'in resemblance to', so the final coordinated nouns کفچ و لفچ are compared to tar — the whole phrase gives a visual simile 'in appearance like pitch, resembling shoes and wrappers.'
segments.s500-g73: چو functions here as a temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیکی (nearness) is used as a noun meaning 'nearness/approach' followed by the genitive noun اژدها (the dragon) to indicate 'near the dragon'. رفت is a past-tense verb 'went/drew (near)' and شاه is the subject 'the king' (typical SOV order is rearranged poetically: temporal clause then verb then subject).
segments.s500-g74: بسان یکی ابر is a simile 'like a single cloud' (بسان = 'like', یکی = 'one/a single'). دیدش is the past tense 'saw' with the enclitic -ش ('its'/'him/her') referring back to the dragon (i.e. 'he saw its...'); سپاه is the noun 'host/army' and is the object of دید(ن) — word order is poetic (simile phrase fronted).
segments.s500-g75: زبانش is 'its tongue' (زبان + enclitic ش for possession). کبود is an adjective 'bluish/azure' placed after the noun (standard Persian adjective follows noun). و links the second coordinated clause: دو چشمش 'its two eyes' with چو + خون forming a simile 'like blood'.
segments.s500-g76: همی is the repetitive/continuous marker used before a verb to show ongoing action in classical Persian ('kept/on repeatedly'); آتش آمد is literally 'fire came' (i.e. 'fire appeared/issued'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', کامش here means 'his mouth' (not abstract desire) and برون is the adverb 'out/forth' — together 'fire kept coming out of his mouth.'
segments.s500-g77: چو is the temporal 'when'. گاو is the direct object 'the cow'. از سر کوه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from the head/top of the mountain' (سر = 'top/summit' + کوه = 'mountain'). بنداختند is a past-tense plural verb; in classical usage it functions as a compound of انداختند 'they threw/cast', but the supplied sense list does not give 'throw', so the precise lexical sense is the archaic compound 'they cast/threw (it down)'.
segments.s500-g78: بران (بر آن) here means 'upon that / against that' (i.e. directed at it). اژدها is 'the dragon'. دل is 'heart' (the target). بپرداختند is a past-tense plural verb; in this context it conveys the act of directing a force/strike at the heart (classical/poetic usage). The supplied sense list doesn't include a simple 'strike/dispatch' meaning, so the verb is best read idiomatically as 'they struck / hurled (it) at'.
segments.s500-g79: فرو برد is a classical compound verb meaning 'took down / swallowed / carried off' (فرو- as a prefix 'down/into' + برد 'took'); in poetry this expresses swift, forceful motion. چون باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چون = 'like', باد = 'wind'). گاو اژدها is 'the dragon's cow' (possessive grouping). Note that برد as a simple lemma in the supplied senses list does not show the verbal sense 'took', so the compound should be read idiomatically.
segments.s500-g80: چو here is another temporal 'when'. آمد is the past 'came'. ز is the poetic 'from'. چنگ means 'claw; grasp' and here refers to warriors' hold. دلیران is the plural 'the brave ones / warriors'. رها is an adjective/participial form 'freed/let go' (used predicatively). Together: 'when it came out of the grasp of the brave, it was free.'
segments.s500-g121: سوی راست پستان is a locative phrase placing something on the right side of the breast: سوی here names the side/direction, followed by the modifier راست and the head noun پستان. چو آن زنان is a simile introducer (چو = “like/as”) plus the demonstrative آن and the plural noun زنان; the clause is elliptical (the copula/verb is omitted) and links as the thing being compared.
segments.s500-g122: بسان یکی نار بر پرنیان is the comparative phrase completing the simile: بسان = “like/as,” یکی nar = “a single flame,” and بر + پرنیان = “upon silk” (بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’). Word order is poetic but straightforward: the metaphorical image (a single fire on silk) modifies the preceding reference to the women.
segments.s500-g123: سوی چپ = “on the left side.” به کردار is a fixed locution meaning “in the manner/role of,” where به is a preposition introducing the manner. جوینده مرد is a noun phrase meaning “one who seeks a man” (جوینده modifies مرد), so the whole chunk reads “on the left, in the demeanor/role of a seeker of a man.”
segments.s500-g124: که introduces a relative clause describing that manner (“who/...”). جوشن is the noun “cuirass/armor.” بپوشد is the classical present (prefix ب- plus present/subjunctive stem) used here descriptively: “(who) puts on/dons.” به‌روز نبرد is an adverbial time phrase “on the day of battle” (به‌روز = ‘on the day’, نبرد = ‘battle’).
segments.s500-g125: چو آمد is a temporal clause “when he came” (چو = “when”). به نزدیک شهر is the directional phrase “to/near the city.” هروم is a noun meaning “uproar/commotion,” placed after شهر for poetic effect: ‘near the city, (there was) commotion.’
segments.s500-g126: سرافراز is an adjectival/predicative adjective meaning “triumphant/proud.” با نامداران روم uses با to mark accompaniment (“with”) and names the companions: نامداران روم = “the renowned men of Rome/Byzantium.” The phrase describes the proud/triumphant stance together with these famed men.
segments.s500-g127: یکی نامه بنوشت = “he wrote a letter” (یکی = “a certain/one”; نامه = “letter”); بنوشت is an older/persianized past form of ‘write’ used in epic diction (equivalent to نوشت). با رسم و داد = “with form/ceremony and justice” (با marks accompaniment/manner; رسم here carries the sense of rite/form/ceremony and داد = justice).
segments.s500-g128: چنانچون بود = “just as/as was” (چنانچون = “as/just as”; بود = past copula “was” used to state the fitting quality). مرد فرخ‌نژاد = “a man of noble/well-born stock” (فرخ‌نژاد = ‘well-born/noble-born’ here rather than merely a family name). The clause means ‘in the manner befitting a noble-born man.’
segments.s500-g225: مرا گرد کافور و خاک سیاه = 'مرا' is a topical/dative-marked expression ('for me', literally 'me' with را). 'گرد' here means 'around' or 'about' and introduces what surrounds or belongs to the speaker; the list 'کافور و خاک سیاه' is the thing(s) being predicated as the speaker's surroundings. Word order is topical: 'مرا' (to me) before the predicate phrase.
segments.s500-g226: همانست و هم بزم و هم رزمگاه = 'همان' ('the same') + copula 'ست' = 'is the same'. The following coordination 'و هم بزم و هم رزمگاه' repeats 'هم' for emphasis: 'also a bāzm (feast/assembly) and also a razmgāh (battlefield)'. The copula applies to the whole coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s500-g227: نه من جنگ را آمدم تازیان = 'نه' negates the clause ('not'). 'من' is the first‑person subject; 'جنگ را' is the definite/marked object/topic ('to war' or 'war' as the affair in question); 'آمدم' is the past tense 'I came/went' (here idiomatically 'I came to war' = 'I went to fight'); 'تازیان' at the end names the manner/standard being denied ('like the Arabs'). The clause denies that the speaker's manner of going to war matches that of the Arabs.
segments.s500-g228: به پیلان و کوس و تبیره زنان = 'به' here marks accompaniment or means ('with/by'), so 'with elephants and drums and ...'. 'پیلان' is 'elephants'; 'کوس' is the war‑drum; 'تبیره' in this context names an instrument or sound associated with parade/war (sense not matched in the provided list); 'زنان' is not 'women' here but the verbal/participial sense 'striking/strikers' (i.e. 'those who strike the instrument'). The whole phrase lists the ostentatious accoutrements the speaker denies using.
segments.s500-g229: سپاهی برین سان که هامون و کوه = 'سپاهی' ('an army/warrior band') followed by the phrase 'برین سان' (a fixed classical expression meaning 'of that sort' or 'such') introduces a relative clause with 'که' ('that/which'). The relative clause's subjects are 'هامون و کوه' (the plain and the mountain) — these are the things affected by the army described.
segments.s500-g230: همی گردد از سم اسپان ستوه = 'همی' is an older continuous/emphatic adverb ('continually/so'), 'گردد' is the stative/impersonal verb 'becomes/is made'. 'از' = 'from/by', 'سم' = 'hoof(s)', 'اسپان' = 'horses' (lit. 'steeds'), and 'ستوه' = 'weary/distressed'. Together: '(they) become weary from the hoofs of the horses' — the predicate of the relative clause.
segments.s500-g231: مرا رای دیدار شهر شماست = 'مرا' marks the experiencer/possessor ('for me' / 'my'), 'رای' here means 'plan/intention', and 'دیدار شهر شما' is a genitive/compound ('the seeing/visit of your city' = 'visiting your city'). The copula 'ست' closes the clause: 'My plan/intention is to visit your city.' Word order places the possessor/topic ('مرا') before the nominal predicate.
segments.s500-g232: گر آیید نزدیک ما هم رواست = 'گر' = conditional 'if'; 'آیید' is 2nd‑person plural present/subjunctive ('you come'); 'نزدیک ما' = 'near us'; 'هم' = 'also'; 'رواست' = 'it is permitted/fitting' (روا + ست). The clause states a conditional permission: 'If you come near us, that too is allowed.'
segments.s500-g201: Literal word order is 'one shame be to-you from-this speech' — a common Persian idiom meaning 'this saying will be a disgrace to you.' باشد is a present/subjunctive copula here expressing a judgment ('would be' / 'is'), ت + را combine to mark the second-person beneficiary/object ('to you' / 'you'); زین is the contracted classical prepositional phrase 'زِ این' ('of/from this') linking سخن ('saying').
segments.s500-g202: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that'); تا هست = 'as long as (it) is/exists' (تا = 'until/so long as', هست = 'exists'); گیتی is the subject 'the world'; نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not become' or 'will not grow'; کهن is the predicate adjective 'old/ancient'. Together: 'that as long as the world exists it will not become old.'
segments.s500-g203: چه خواهی que-question 'what do you want / what will you do' followed by a complement clause introduced by که ('that/that you'); با + نامداران روم = 'with the renowned men of Rum' (با = 'with', نامداران = 'renowned ones', روم = the land/people of Rum i.e. Byzantium). The phrase sets up the company/companions in question.
segments.s500-g204: Two verbs in sequence express the intended action: بیایی is a subjunctive/optative second-person form of 'come' ('you come' / 'you should come'), and بگردی is the subjunctive of گشتن meaning 'to roam/wander/turn about.' به مرز هروم = 'to the border/edge of commotion/uproar' (به = 'to/toward', مرز = 'border', هروم = 'uproar/commotion'). The sequence asks whether the addressee will come and wander to that frontier.
segments.s500-g205: چو here = 'when/if'; با راستی باشی is a subjunctive/conditional clause 'if you are with uprightness / if you are honest' (با = 'with', راستی = 'uprightness/truth', باشی = subjunctive 'you be'). و مردمی attaches 'and (among) the people' — مردمی functions adverbially/relationally 'among the populace / as far as the people are concerned.'
segments.s500-g206: نبینی is a negative present/subjunctive 'you do not see' (or 'you will see no one'); جز از = 'except from/of' (here 'except'); خوبی و خرمی = 'goodness and delight/mirth' (parallel nouns). The clause means 'you will see nothing but goodness and mirth.'
segments.s500-g207: به پیش تو آریم is literal 'we bring/shall place before you' (به پیش = 'in front of/before', تو = 'you'); آریم is an archaic/poetic first-person-plural verb form 'we bring/put' (classical Persian form). چندان سپاه = 'so great an army/host' (چندان = 'so much/so great', سپاه = 'army/host').
segments.s500-g208: که introduces the result clause: 'that' تیره شود = 'becomes darkened' (تیره = 'dark', شود = subjunctive/passive 'becomes/is made'); بر تو = 'over/upon you' (direction/target); خورشید و ماه = 'the sun and the moon.' Together: 'so that the sun and moon grow dark over you.'
segments.s500-g25: This is a prepositional location phrase + noun phrase: 'بران' is a contracted classical form meaning 'بر آن' (upon/on that) and precedes the place noun 'کوه' (mountain). 'مردم بدی اندکی' is a postnominal modifier order common in Persian: 'مردم' (people) + adjective 'بدی' (evil/wicked) + quantifier 'اندکی' (a few). Together: 'upon that mountain (were) a few wicked people.'
segments.s500-g26: 'شب تیره' is a noun + adjective ('dark night'). 'زیشان' = 'زی ایشان' functions as 'of them / among them' and attaches to the clause. 'نماندی' (from ماندن) here means 'was left / remained' and with the final 'یکی' ('one') placed after the verb yields the negative sense 'not one remained' (classical word order places the numeral/emphatic element after the verb).
segments.s500-g27: 'بپرسید' is the past tense verb 'asked' (third-person) and appears before the rest of the clause in classical word order. 'ازیشان' = 'از ایشان' means 'from them / of them' (the people he questioned). 'سکندر' is the subject name placed after the object phrase (common in older Persian). 'که' introduces the following interrogative 'راه' (way).
segments.s500-g28: 'کدامست' is an interrogative 'which is it?' referring back to 'راه'. The conjunction 'و' links the next question. 'چون' here means 'how' and governs the verb 'راند' ('to drive/lead'); 'باید' expresses necessity ('must / should'), and 'سپاه' (army) is the patient/affected element placed at the end. The clause order is characteristic of classical Persian: question word → verb → modal → object.
segments.s500-g29: 'همه' (all/everyone) is the subject. 'یکسره' is an adverb here meaning 'at once / straightaway' and modifies the verb. 'خواندند' is the past plural 'they called/sang/uttered', and 'آفرین' is the exclamation they uttered ('bravo' / 'well done'). The verb-subject-adverb-object ordering reflects classical usage.
segments.s500-g30: The phrase is a vocative/exclamatory address. 'که‌ای' is a poetic/colloquial combination functioning like a vocative particle ('O' or introducing the address). segments.s500-g167: اگر is the conditional 'if'. لشکر is the subject 'an army/host'. آری here is an archaic/poetic form functioning as a verb meaning 'comes/arrives' (in classical Persian one encounters such forms), but that verbal sense is not present in the supplied senses list, so it is marked accordingly. به شهر 'to/into the city' uses به as the preposition, and هروم is a noun 'uproar/commotion', describing the manner of entry.
segments.s500-g168: نبینی is a negative-subjunctive/optative form 'you would not see' (here expressing the consequence in the conditional). ز introduces the cause 'because of / from' (poetic z). نعل 'horseshoe' and پی 'foot/track' (here 'tracks/hoofmarks') are coordinated by و, and اسپ 'steed/horse' modifies پی (the tracks of steeds). بوم 'ground/native soil' is the affected object: 'you would not see the ground because of horseshoes and the tracks of horses.'
segments.s500-g129: به عنوان introduces a formal heading or pretext ('in the name/under the title of'). segments.s500-g130: سوی is the preposition 'toward'. آنک is the relative/demonstrative 'that which/where', linking to the following clause. دارند is a present plural 'have/keep' (subject is contextually understood). مرز هروم is a noun phrase 'the border of uproar' or 'uproar at the frontier' with هروم ('uproar/commotion') qualifying مرز.

segments.s500-g132: کزویست is a fused relative expression 'which is from him' (literally 'that which is from him') attributing the following qualities to the Creator. بخشایش و داد و مهر is a simple nominal list ('forgiveness, justice, and kindness/affection'); there is no finite verb — a common verbless attribution in headings.
segments.s500-g133: هرانکس که ... is a relative clause 'whoever that ...'. که introduces the clause; دارد is the present/presentive 'has/possesses'. روانش خرد is a possessive structure where روانش ('his soul') is linked to خرد ('wisdom') — 'wisdom in his soul'.
segments.s500-g134: جهان را is the definite object marked by را. به عمری is an adverbial phrase 'for a lifetime' (به = for/to, عمری = for a lifetime). همی is the archaic habitual/presentive particle often used with verbs in Ferdowsi; بسپرد is the verb 'to entrust/hand over' (3rd sing. present/subjunctive sense in classical usage), placed at the end of the clause as typical in Persian.
segments.s500-g135: شنید here functions as an exhortation/imperative-like 'listen/hear' (archaic usage possible). آنک introduces a nominal relative 'that which/what', and ما ... کرده‌ایم is a perfect construction 'we have done' describing actions in the world (در جهان = 'in the world').
segments.s500-g136: سر مهتری is a compound nominal 'the head/chief of preeminence' (سر here = 'chief/leader' and مهتری = 'preeminence'). بر کجا برده‌ایم is a question 'to where have we carried [it]?' with بر functioning as a directional preposition 'to/upon', کجا 'where', and برده‌ایم the perfect 'we have carried'. segments.s500-g169: بی‌اندازه is an adverb meaning "excessively/immeasurably" placed before the locative phrase در شهر ما (in our city). برزنست is a poetic/preserved predicate formed with an older copula ending -ست (≈ است) and here functions as the main verb phrase "there is/there are ..." — the whole segment reads literally "excessively in our city there is ...".
segments.s500-g170: بهر introduces a distributional sense 'for/for each'; برزنی is the item being distributed (here effectively 'for each man' in context, though the provided lemma senses do not match this use); بر is the usual preposition 'on/for', and هزاران زنست is a quantitative predicate "is thousands of women" where زنست functions adjectivally/predicatively (womanly/being women). segments.s500-g171: همه شب is a temporal adverbial 'every night'. به خفتان (به + خفتان) marks the target/objects of hostility — 'upon/against sleepers' — followed by the noun جنگ 'war' and the verb-like form اندریم (archaic 1st‑pl. construction with اندر- + -یم) meaning 'we are in / we wage' (i.e. 'we wage war').
